title: Code
tag: The concepts beneath the Code letter
scan: What you will learn: The eight Stalwart file types — init, model, biz, api, render, config, state, events — and the structural contract each one encodes: a single responsibility, a fixed list of what it must not contain

Why it matters: Stalwart is the production implementation of modules 00–10 — it converts architectural principles into a file-system convention an AI agent can be instructed to follow, making every generated feature structurally identical and reviewable

The distinction between a naive prompt and a Stalwart prompt is the difference between working code and architecturally correct code — the prompt carries the architecture so the AI cannot invent its own
The architect's role in a Stalwart project is contract definition and code review, not code authorship — this is the correct division of labour for AI-augmented teams
Stalwart is opinionated by design — deviations must be documented as Architecture Decision Records, not silent exceptions

blocks.html: Before Stalwart (one implementation) comes the conceptual vocabulary the implementation is built from. Thirteen short modules, each collapsed by default — expand the ones you need, or dip in via the sidebar. The anchors still work; clicking a deep link auto-opens the matching module.
How to use · Modules cluster naturally into foundations (00–02b), verbs & events (03–04), state & collections (05–07), and discipline (08–10). Read sequentially or jump in.

Optional — promote when the core file is overloaded · config.js · flags, endpoints, thresholds (no other file hard-codes these) · state.js · reactive runtime store (model.js holds the shape; state.js the current value) · events.js · event vocabulary and dispatcher wiring (biz.js still owns decisions)

AI-generated code that does not conform to a file structure convention produces PRs that take 3× longer to review — because the reviewer must reconstruct context (what does this file do? where does its state live? who calls it?) on every PR. Stalwart answers those questions before the reviewer opens a file.

DEPT® field pattern — add a Stalwart file-type comment at the top of every generated file: // stalwart: model or // stalwart: render. A CI grep on this comment catches misplaced logic before human review. Cost: one comment per file. Benefit: 20 minutes of review time saved per PR.

Treating biz.js as a catch-all for "business logic and everything else." The biz file owns orchestration only — it calls model and api, it does not transform data or render output. A biz file with more than 80 lines is almost always doing work that belongs in model or render. This is the most common Stalwart violation on live DEPT® projects.
